network.overall_strategy: This network targets proline through chemically more defensible C3-to-C5 chemistry centered on hydrothermal production of pyruvate and acetate, surface generation of succinate, oxaloacetate, aspartate, and glutamate-family intermediates, and biochemical closure through glutamate semialdehyde / 1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C). To avoid the failures of earlier attempts, the network does not rely on implausible formate-to-formaldehyde-to-HCN sequences or incorrect Strecker assignments from pyruvate. Instead, it uses convergent routes to pyruvate, oxaloacetate, aspartate, glutamate, glutamate semialdehyde, and P5C, with multiple environmental entries and ten valid reaction pathways to the target. Chirality is treated as arising during late-stage mineral-surface or compartment-assisted resolution/amplification; the immediate chemical precursor is racemic proline unless otherwise noted, and L-proline is designated as the selected target in the final prebiotic enrichment step.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of CO2 to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents that desorb as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of CO2 to formate on Fe-S minerals is among the better supported vent chemistries and provides a robust C1 hub for prebiotic carbon fixation analogs (Roldan et al., 2015; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal acetate formation from CO2/formate equivalents
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise reductive carbon fixation on Fe-S surfaces yields C2 acetyl-like intermediates that hydrolyze to acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate is a plausible hydrothermal product via iron-sulfur-mediated CO2 fixation analogs and serves as a realistic C2 feedstock into proto-metabolic networks (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 1997; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced C1/C2 carbon
reactions.mechanism: Sequential carbonyl insertion and reduction on mineral surfaces generates a C3 alpha-keto acid analogous to pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from CO2-derived feedstocks under hydrothermal or metal-catalyzed fixation conditions is less certain than formate/acetate but remains a plausible extension of reductive protometabolic chemistry; included here as a cautiously supported hydrothermal hub because alpha-keto acid generation has experimental support in protometabolic analog systems (Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate

conditions.other: evaporitic pool with wet-dry concentration, Fe2+ available, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Carboxylation of an enolized pyruvate-like intermediate gives oxaloacetate under concentrated, mineral-buffered conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic oxaloacetate synthesis is challenging, but pyruvate carboxylation analogs are commonly invoked in protometabolic reconstructions; this step is somewhat speculative yet chemically more reasonable than earlier carbon-mismatched proposals and is needed to connect the C3 hub to aspartate chemistry (Muchowska et al., 2019; Keller et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive carboxylation of acetate to succinate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal system with Fe0 as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Successive reductive carbon additions to C2 acetyl equivalents generate C4 dicarboxylates represented here by succinate.
reactions.reasoning: Intermediates and products of the reductive TCA analog, including succinate, can emerge under metal-driven non-enzymatic conditions; this offers a better grounded C4 entry than unsupported amino acid homologations (Muchowska et al., 2017; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation of succinate to oxaloacetate via mineral-assisted dicarboxylate activation

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow pool, anoxic overall, photocatalytic oxidation equivalent

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic and mineral-assisted oxidation of a C4 dicarboxylate pool feeds keto-dicarboxylate formation represented by oxaloacetate.
reactions.reasoning: This oxidation is a network-closing step and is moderately speculative, but TiO2-mediated photoredox chemistry in surface environments can alter carboxylate oxidation states and is used here to create convergence onto oxaloacetate (Guzman and Martin, 2010; Keller et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.other: prebiotic pool, metal ions and repeated hydration-dehydration cycles

reactions.mechanism: Non-enzymatic amination/transamination of the alpha-keto acid gives the corresponding alpha-amino acid aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-keto acids readily connect to amino acids by non-enzymatic transamination or reductive amination chemistry under prebiotic conditions, and oxaloacetate-to-aspartate is one of the most metabolically coherent such links (Matsumoto et al., 1990; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation at the alpha-keto group followed by reduction yields glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Non-enzymatic reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is prebiotically plausible and provides the direct biochemical entry to glutamate-family amino acids required for proline synthesis (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Chain-extension of aspartate to alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: condensed pool with repeated heating-cooling, proto-metabolic carbon insertion

reactions.mechanism: Aspartate-family carbon skeleton undergoes deamination/carboxymethylation-like homologation to the C5 alpha-keto acid pool represented by alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: This is the most speculative connection in the network but is included as a necessary proto-metabolic bridge from C4 aspartate chemistry to the C5 glutamate family. It is explicitly speculative, though motivated by the need for network continuity and by evidence that non-enzymatic metabolic analogs can interconvert central-acid skeletons (Muchowska et al., 2019; Keller et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Carbonyl reduction of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate semialdehyde

conditions.other: reducing interface with Fe2+/sulfide and compartmental concentration

reactions.mechanism: Selective partial reduction of one terminal carboxylate-equivalent of alpha-ketoglutarate yields the aldehyde-containing glutamate semialdehyde pool.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic access to glutamate semialdehyde is a recognized challenge; this step is speculative but chemically more targeted than the previously criticized vague glutamate reductions. It reflects the need for a reduced C5 amino aldehyde before cyclization to P5C/proline (Said and Fuchs, 1973 for biochemical precedent; speculative prebiotic adaptation).

reactions.name: Cyclization of glutamate semialdehyde to 1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate

conditions.other: spontaneous intramolecular condensation in concentrated solution

reactions.mechanism: The amino aldehyde cyclizes spontaneously by intramolecular Schiff-base/hemiaminal formation to the five-membered imine ring of P5C.
reactions.reasoning: This is a chemically strong step: glutamate semialdehyde is known to cyclize spontaneously to P5C, making it the most credible closure into the proline ring system (Vogel and Davis, 1952; biochemical consensus).

reactions.name: Reduction of 1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate to proline with late-stage L-enrichment

conditions.other: reducing prebiotic microenvironment; mineral-surface or eutectic selection enriches L-enantiomer from racemate

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the ring imine gives proline; subsequent asymmetric adsorption/resolution or amplification enriches the L-enantiomer.
reactions.reasoning: P5C reduction to proline is chemically straightforward in principle, though specific prebiotic reductants remain uncertain. The stereochemical assignment to L-proline reflects a late-stage enrichment/selection process rather than direct asymmetric synthesis, addressing earlier criticism about unsupported L-selectivity (Blackmond, 2010; Hazen et al., 2001 for mineral-mediated chiral enrichment concepts).

reactions.name: Surface wet-dry cyclodehydration of glutamate to pyroglutamate

conditions.other: drying lagoon or geothermal field, repeated wet-dry cycles

reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular condensation of glutamate under dehydration conditions gives the lactam pyroglutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyroglutamate formation from glutamate by heating/dehydration is chemically well precedented and offers a realistic surface branch into the proline ring family (Fox and Harada, 1958; Rode, 1999).

reactions.name: Reductive conversion of pyroglutamate to 1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate
reactions.mechanism: Partial reduction/tautomerization of the lactam ring gives the imine-containing P5C pool.
reactions.reasoning: This transformation is more speculative than direct GSA cyclization but provides a plausible alternative convergence route from dehydrated glutamate chemistry into the same immediate precursor of proline.

reactions.name: Alternative transamination of pyruvate-derived carbon to alpha-ketoglutarate pool

conditions.other: mixed hydrothermal-surface feedstocks in a concentrated pool

reactions.mechanism: C3 and C4 central-carbon intermediates participate in non-enzymatic carbon redistribution yielding the C5 alpha-ketoglutarate pool.
reactions.reasoning: This is explicitly speculative, but it creates a second convergence route to alpha-ketoglutarate from independently plausible pyruvate and succinate hubs, satisfying network redundancy while keeping carbon accounting coherent.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to proline core route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced hydrothermally to formate, acetate, and pyruvate, then carboxylated through oxaloacetate and aspartate into the glutamate-family route to proline.

pathways.name: Succinate-oxaloacetate convergence route
pathways.description: Acetate is extended hydrothermally to succinate, oxidized at the surface to oxaloacetate, then passed through aspartate and the glutamate-family branch to proline.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-to-alpha-ketoglutarate shortcut route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and succinate pools combine through a proto-metabolic redistribution step to alpha-ketoglutarate, bypassing oxaloacetate/aspartate.

pathways.name: Direct glutamate semialdehyde route from alpha-ketoglutarate
pathways.description: Once alpha-ketoglutarate accumulates from the aspartate route, direct reduction to glutamate semialdehyde and spontaneous cyclization yield the immediate proline precursor.

pathways.name: Glutamate-first route
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate is first aminated to glutamate, then reduced onward through glutamate semialdehyde to P5C and proline.

pathways.name: Pyroglutamate detour route
pathways.description: Glutamate formed from alpha-ketoglutarate undergoes surface cyclodehydration to pyroglutamate, which is then reduced into the P5C pool before final reduction to proline.

pathways.name: Succinate-pyroglutamate route
pathways.description: The succinate branch feeds oxaloacetate and aspartate, then glutamate is diverted through a dry-surface pyroglutamate intermediate before reduction to proline.

pathways.name: Formate-supported aspartate-to-proline route
pathways.description: Formate participates as a C1 support in the homologation from aspartate to alpha-ketoglutarate, then the canonical glutamate-semialdehyde route reaches proline.

pathways.name: Dual hydrothermal feed to alpha-ketoglutarate
pathways.description: Independent hydrothermal generation of pyruvate and succinate converges on alpha-ketoglutarate, which then proceeds through glutamate and P5C to proline.

pathways.name: Maximum-convergence environmental route
pathways.description: This path uses hydrothermal formate and acetate production, hydrothermal pyruvate, surface oxaloacetate generation, biochemical aspartate and glutamate-family chemistry, and final biochemical reduction to the selected L-enantiomer.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The route is topologically coherent and reaches proline, but several core chemical steps are weak. CO2-to-formate and glutamate-semialdehyde cyclization are plausible, while pyruvate formation is only moderately supported. The major liabilities are surface reductive carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate, aspartate-to-alpha-ketoglutarate chain extension with formate, and direct reduction of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate semialdehyde. These steps make the sequence chemically fragile even though the environmental handoff is conceptually reasonable.
suggestions: Replace rxn_004 with a more defensible oxaloacetate-generating route, such as malate/fumarate-mediated carboxylation-oxidation chemistry if supported
Replace rxn_009 with a documented pathway from C4 intermediates to alpha-ketoglutarate, likely involving additional C1/C2 activated intermediates rather than formate alone
Route alpha-ketoglutarate through glutamate first, then dehydration/reduction to glutamate semialdehyde or P5C

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway benefits from convergence through succinate and oxaloacetate, but the central dicarboxylate chemistry is poorly grounded. Reductive carboxylation of acetate to succinate under hydrothermal conditions and oxidation of succinate to oxaloacetate on a surface are both speculative and likely major bottlenecks. Downstream conversion from aspartate to alpha-ketoglutarate remains especially weak.
suggestions: Introduce fumarate or malate as explicit intermediates if a succinate-to-oxaloacetate sequence is intended
Avoid direct acetate-to-succinate claims unless supported by specific mineral-catalyzed literature
Use glutamate-based chemistry to access P5C rather than alpha-ketoglutarate direct reduction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The shortcut is appealing topologically, but the chemistry of pyruvate plus succinate yielding alpha-ketoglutarate is not credible as written. Reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate is plausible, and glutamate to pyroglutamate under wet-dry cycling is acceptable. However, pyroglutamate-to-P5C reduction is poorly justified, leaving the route unsupported at its core shortcut step.
suggestions: Remove rxn_015 or decompose it into chemically explicit aldol/carboxylation/redox steps with named intermediates
Use glutamate semialdehyde as the immediate precursor to P5C instead of invoking pyroglutamate reduction
Clarify whether pyroglutamate is a side product or productive branch

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is essentially the core direct semialdehyde branch and inherits the same weaknesses as the pyruvate-oxaloacetate-aspartate route. The glutamate semialdehyde to P5C step is strong, but obtaining glutamate semialdehyde directly from alpha-ketoglutarate remains chemically implausible without prior amination and selective reduction chemistry. The target is reached, but by a path with low mechanistic support.
suggestions: Insert glutamate explicitly before glutamate semialdehyde and make that branch primary rather than optional
Rebuild the alpha-ketoglutarate-forming chemistry around more credible C4 to C5 transformations

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the more coherent pathways because it goes through glutamate, a defensible proline-family precursor. Still, it depends on weak upstream chemistry to generate alpha-ketoglutarate and on an uncertain productive role for pyroglutamate. The environmental progression from hydrothermal carbon fixation to biochemical amino-acid chemistry is better than in most other paths, but the chain-extension chemistry remains the key unresolved issue.
suggestions: Make glutamate semialdehyde formation proceed from glutamate rather than pyroglutamate reduction
Strengthen the oxaloacetate/aspartate/alpha-ketoglutarate segment with literature-supported intermediates
Specify mineral agents for surface reactions, which are currently absent from the summary

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pyroglutamate detour is plausible as a side dehydration product under wet-dry surface conditions, but much less convincing as a productive route toward P5C and proline. Upstream chemistry is the same as in the glutamate-first path and remains the limiting factor. This pathway is connected and chemically interpretable, but not strongly supported as a dominant route.
suggestions: Treat pyroglutamate as a branch or sink rather than a mainline precursor unless stronger support is available
Use glutamate semialdehyde/P5C interconversion as the principal productive step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route combines two speculative segments: the succinate-to-oxaloacetate branch and the pyroglutamate-to-P5C branch. While the network remains connected and convergent, too many individual transformations lack strong prebiotic support for this to be considered a robust pathway to proline.
suggestions: Remove one speculative detour at a time and focus on the strongest branch through glutamate semialdehyde
Introduce more chemically explicit dicarboxylate redox intermediates if retaining the succinate branch

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This path is among the better ones because it uses the more defensible glutamate/P5C/proline logic rather than relying on pyroglutamate alone. However, the aspartate-to-alpha-ketoglutarate step remains highly problematic, and direct alpha-ketoglutarate-to-glutamate semialdehyde reduction is also poorly supported. Better than the succinate or pyroglutamate-heavy alternatives, but still below approval quality.
suggestions: Substitute a literature-supported alpha-ketoglutarate synthesis route
Replace direct rxn_010 with glutamate-based semialdehyde formation chemistry
Detail how L-enrichment occurs, since target is specifically L-proline

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual hydrothermal feed is conceptually attractive, but both routes into alpha-ketoglutarate are weak. Acetate-to-succinate and pyruvate-plus-succinate-to-alpha-ketoglutarate are especially problematic. Downstream semialdehyde cyclization is strong, but upstream route quality is too low for confidence.
suggestions: Retain hydrothermal feed only to formate/acetate/pyruvate and rebuild C5 access on the surface or in pooled chemistry
Eliminate lumped alpha-ketoglutarate shortcut chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This maximum-convergence route scores best on network architecture and environmental integration but not on chemistry. It demonstrates strong cross-environment connectivity, yet relies on too many low-confidence steps concentrated around succinate/oxaloacetate/alpha-ketoglutarate construction and pyroglutamate reduction. The route is a good systems sketch, not yet a chemically convincing synthesis pathway.
suggestions: Preserve the convergent architecture but prune speculative chemistry
Add explicit, literature-backed intermediates to connect C4 and C5 amino-acid chemistry
Ensure all surface and hydrothermal reactions include named mineral agents in the full specification

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong in topology and systems design but only moderate to weak in chemical defensibility. Positives include adequate size, clear hub structure, multiple convergence points, full connectivity, and a credible broad environmental storyline from hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface concentration to biochemical closure. The hydrothermal entry chemistry around CO2 reduction to formate and acetate is plausible, and the late-stage glutamate semialdehyde to P5C to proline logic is chemically sound. However, the network is undermined by several key unsupported transformations: acetate to succinate, succinate to oxaloacetate in one step, pyruvate to oxaloacetate under the stated surface reductive carboxylation framing, aspartate plus formate to alpha-ketoglutarate, direct alpha-ketoglutarate reduction to glutamate semialdehyde, and pyruvate plus succinate to alpha-ketoglutarate. In addition, the provided summary does not specify mineral agents for each hydrothermal and surface reaction, which is a formal requirement. Literature alignment cannot be scored highly because no reaction-level citations are supplied. Overall, the network is a good connected conceptual map to proline, but not yet an approved prebiotic synthesis network.
